title: Will Japan break the Treasury bond market?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
our skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Friday, July 10th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, will
Japan break the Treasury bond market?
The short answer is no, but it will
certainly try, however inadvertently.
Japan's trying represents a structurally
bullish dynamic for risk assets. In
terms of analytical nuance, Japan's
finance minister urged pension funds to
tilt their allocations increasingly
towards domestic assets, lifting the yen
from four-decade lows and rallying JGBs.
Fielding questions about how strategic
investments would benefit Japanese
citizens, she made seemingly innocuous
comments that could have major domestic
economic and geopolitical implications
because Japan's
pension fund, government pension fund,
ranks among the world's largest
pensions, holding $1.8 trillion in
assets, and Japan is the third largest
net international investment surplus
economy in the world at $3.5 trillion.
Recall that Prime Minister Sanae
Takaichi last month proposed a $2.3
trillion uh fiscal policy uh strategy
across 14 years with over 25% dedicated
to AI and chips. Its scale is unmatched.
The prospect of durable reflationary
fiscal policy in Japan represents a
durable negative demand shock for US
Treasury bonds, especially in the
context of a BOJ that was recently given
latitude to further normalize Japanese
monetary policy in the upcoming economic
and fiscal agenda. We presciently
identified these dynamics roughly 3
years ago amid the advent of our
inaugural Investing Don't Afford Turning
Regime presentation. Refer to our fourth
turning FAQ for more details.
Japan is the world's third largest net
international investment surplus
economy. Any material moderation of its
capital outflows will have a material
impact on global financial markets. The
30-year high in long-term JGB yields and
40-year low in the Japanese yen versus
the US dollar are telltale signs that
Japan Japan requires more of its own
capital to finance Prime Minister
Kishida's ambitious fiscal policy
agenda.
Japan being forced to increasingly
capitalize its own sovereign debt market
due to pre-persistent reflationary
fiscal policy is part and parcel of our
core thesis that there is a
geopolitically driven supply-demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
aka paradigm A.
Europe remilitarizing, China decoupling,
and BRICS members uh generally
disfavoring US dollar hegemony are key
drivers of the disequilibrium as well.
The trailing 10-year growth rate of
global savings is among the lowest on
record and well below its long-run mean.
This dynamic is unlikely to meaningfully
or durably improve given the ongoing
shift to a multipolar world and all the
added fiscal expenditures on defense and
infrastructure that entails. The
reversal of global capital flow
dynamics, the protracted deceleration of
global savings growth, and the
protracted acceleration of US fiscal
expenditures combine to leave the US
Treasury in a precarious position with
respect to capitalizing its debt stock.
Treasury Secretary Yellen must cap
finance nearly $12 trillion over the
next 12 months, more than double the
Treasury's capitalization needs just
prior to COVID. The persistence and
likely deepening of the geopolitically
driven supply-demand imbalance in the
Treasury bond market is why the Fed has
no choice but to continue monetizing US
sovereign debt over the long term. A key
reason why we believe Kevin Warsh was
nominated to succeed Jay Powell as Fed
chair is because he's the most credible
dove in hawk's clothing.
As always, I'll wrap up with a question
from our community here, transitioning
to our 42 macro dashboard. This one's uh
titled Mag 7.
Says on a gross adjusted EP on a gross
adjusted basis,
uh the Mag 7s are the cheapest they've
ever been in over 10 years. This is the
AI-driven EPS power in their hand. Uh
Darius is saying no, avoid Mag 7. I'm a
little confused why. Uh rather than give
my answer, I'm just going to repeat the
answer verbatim from a number another
member here. Uh says, because the Mag 7
will be a source of funds, meaning
institutions will sell mega cap tech
stocks to raise cash to buy other
assets. Mega cap tech is the market's at
the money position for a new regime.
From what Darius has said, it is
something to do with fundamentals, but
more so to do with the nature of the new
regime, liquidity, bookable gains,
concentration risk, and a lot of other
stuff. So, uh you know, excellent
answer. Uh as a as a educator, uh it,
you know, warms my heart and and brings
a a tear to my eye when I see uh
members, you know, one helping each
other come up their own investing
journey. You know, help each other, you
know, up the respective investing
journeys. And you know, when they get
started answering questions to this
degree, you know, you know, it just it
lets me know that I'm doing my job as an
educator and it's something I'm I'm
really uh thankful for
uh this morning. So, let's just uh
quickly wrap up. Let's get a little
quick check in with uh MAGS 7. Here,
where we at, MAGS? Looks like Dr. Mo is
signaling for MAGS right now. So, it's
still bullish. Uh he obviously uh pretty
pretty low pretty pretty strong off the
highs, but you know, so in absolute
sense, you know, MAGS 7 is fine, but in
a relative sense other factors in the
market, uh you know, it's been lagging
pretty pretty tremendously uh in recent
months. And that's exactly what we
called for when we outlined the theme
last fall. So, no change to the theme.
It's not going to go anywhere in a
linear straight line, particularly if we
go into a risk off market regime at some
point over the next two to four months.
Then it's highly likely that investors
will rotate back into MAGS 7 because
they'll be they'll be closing their long
positions and covering their shorts and
their underweights
taking down their gross exposure, which
will, you know, benefit the MAGS 7, but
ultimately we think this is a longer
term thing that has, you know, legs well
out into the next, you know, one, two,
perhaps three, four, five years. So,
we'll we'll see about that. We'll wrap
it up there. Uh actually, before we wrap
it up there, we got our July macro
scouting report presentation coming out
this afternoon. So, I'm very excited uh
to present that. We are at a massive
crossroads from a macro standpoint right
now. I don't think investors realize
just how many crosscurrents are building
up right now that can produce some
pretty explosive outcomes in the second
half of the year. So, uh if you're not
aware of those crosscurrents, uh prepare
to get aware uh this afternoon. So,
thanks thanks in advance everyone who's
tuning in for that. Everyone have a
great weekend. We'll catch back here on
Monday. Cheers.
